did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically training a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and applying fine-tuning on pre-trained models (ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and DenseNet101) to classify skin cancer types (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) using a dataset of 10,015 skin lesion images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details a specific methodology involving the training of a custom CNN and fine-tuning of pre-trained models (ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet101) on a dataset of 10,015 images, followed by the reporting of new results (e.g., VGG16 achieving 86% test accuracy). While it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new empirical findings rather than summarize prior literature.

title: Determination of Skin Cancer Types with Deep Learning Methods and Patient Management
journal: Liaquat National Journal of Cancer Care
brief_summary: This study aimed to classify skin cancer types using deep learning methods to support patient diagnosis. The researchers utilized a publicly available dataset of 10,015 skin lesion images, dividing it into 80% for training/validation and 20% for testing. The methodology involved training a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and applying fine-tuning to pre-trained models including ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and DenseNet101. Model performance was evaluated using test accuracy and F1-score metrics. The VGG16 model achieved the highest performance with 96% training accuracy and 86% test accuracy. The authors conclude that deep learning, particularly transfer learning with VGG16, can effectively accelerate skin cancer diagnosis and serve as a clinical decision support tool.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions into 7 specific categories: Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, and Dermatofibroma. The models (custom CNN, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet101) predict discrete class labels, with performance evaluated using test accuracy and F1-score. The application displays probabilities for each potential cancer type to support diagnosis.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The study utilized a secondary dataset of 10,015 images from this source.

use.training: 80% of the dataset was divided into training and validation sets for training the custom CNN and fine-tuning pre-trained models (ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet101).
use.validation: Part of the 80% split used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset was used as the test set to assess model performance using test accuracy and test F1-score metrics.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of the VGG16 model with the baseline CNN and other pre-trained models using cross-validation accuracy scores.
To determine if the observed improvement in classification accuracy was statistically significant and unlikely to be due to random variation.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in classification accuracy between the VGG16 model and the baseline CNN or other pre-trained models; any observed improvement is due to random variation.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The VGG16 model's classification accuracy is statistically significantly superior to the baseline CNN and other pre-trained models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The p-values were consistently below 0.05, indicating that the VGG16 model's superior performance was statistically significant and unlikely to be due to random variation, confirming a genuine performance advantage.

HAM10000.notes: VGG16 achieved the highest test accuracy (0.86) and F1 score (0.81). A paired t-test on cross-validation accuracy scores showed p-values consistently below 0.05, indicating VGG16's superior performance was statistically significant compared to the baseline CNN and other pre-trained models.

conclusions: The VGG16 fine-tuned deep learning model achieved the highest performance among the tested architectures, with 96% training accuracy, 86% test accuracy, and an F1-score of 0.81.

Pre-trained convolutional neural networks (ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and DenseNet101) demonstrated considerably higher test accuracy and F1-scores compared to the custom CNN trained from scratch, highlighting the beneficial effect of transfer learning.

The superior performance of the VGG16 model over the baseline CNN and other pre-trained models was statistically significant (p < 0.05), as determined by a paired t-test on cross-validation accuracy scores.

The relatively small performance differences among VGG16, VGG19, and ResNet50 suggest that the applicability of feature representations from large natural-image datasets is more critical for reliable diagnostic performance than the depth of the architecture.

Deep learning methods, particularly transfer learning with VGG16, can effectively accelerate skin cancer diagnosis and serve as a clinical decision support tool to aid practitioners in identifying lesion types and deciding on further diagnostic tests like biopsies.

The custom CNN model showed signs of overfitting, with training accuracy exceeding 90% while validation accuracy remained below 70%, resulting in lower test performance (69% accuracy, 0.66 F1-score) compared to transfer learning models.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (HAM10000) without external validation across different patient populations or imaging devices, limiting generalizability.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with Melanocytic nevi comprising 67% of samples (6,705 cases) while Vascular lesions and Dermatofibroma are severely underrepresented (142 and 115 cases respectively).

The custom CNN model showed signs of overfitting, with training accuracy exceeding 90% while validation accuracy remained below 70%, resulting in lower test performance (69% accuracy) compared to transfer learning models.
No data augmentation techniques were employed in the methodology, which may have contributed to the limited generalization performance observed.
The study acknowledges that successful deployment of such systems requires addressing class balance, lesion diversity, and appropriate regularization/augmentation techniques.

The proposed application is positioned as a clinical decision support tool to aid practitioners rather than a standalone diagnostic system, emphasizing the need for further diagnostic tests like biopsies based on probability outputs.
